title: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms

items.subtopic: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms
items.title: Genetic Basis of Phenotypic Heterogeneity in MPNs
items.content: The phenotypic heterogeneity among chronic myeloproliferative neoplasms (MPNs) has a genetic basis, with different disorders associated with specific chromosomal translocations or mutations. CML is linked to the [9;22][q34;q11] translocation, CNL with CSF3R mutation and t(15;19), while CEL involves deletions or translocations affecting PDGFRα and related genes. In contrast, PV, PMF, and ET are characterized by mutations activating JAK2.
keyPoints: CML is caused by the [9;22][q34;q11] translocation.
CNL involves CSF3R mutation and t(15;19) translocation.
CEL is associated with deletions or translocations involving PDGFRα, FIP1L1, PDGFRβ, FGFR1, and PCM1-JAK2 genes.
PV, PMF, and ET are characterized by JAK2 mutations.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms

items.subtopic: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms
items.title: Natural History and Transformation Rates in MPNs
items.content: The natural histories of CML, CNL, and CEL are typically measured in years with a high rate of leukemic transformation. In contrast, PV, PMF, and ET have longer durations, usually decades, with uncommon transformation to acute leukemia without chemotherapy.
keyPoints: CML, CNL, and CEL have natural histories measured in years.
High rate of leukemic transformation for CML, CNL, and CEL.
PV, PMF, and ET are measured over decades.
Transformation to acute leukemia is uncommon in PV, PMF, and ET without chemotherapy.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms

items.title: JAK2 V617F Mutation in PV Pathogenesis
items.content: The JAK2 V617F mutation plays a central role in the pathogenesis of polycythemia vera (PV) by causing constitutive activation of the tyrosine kinase, which leads to erythropoietin hypersensitivity and increased blood cell production.
keyPoints: More than 95% of PV patients express the JAK2 V617F mutation.
The mutation causes constitutive activation of JAK2, leading to erythropoietin-independent erythroid colony formation and resistance to apoptosis.
JAK2 V617F is located on chromosome 9p, with loss of heterozygosity in this region occurring in ~60% of PV patients.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · POLYCYTHEMIA VERA | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Cytogenetic Abnormalities and JAK2 Mutations
items.content: While nonrandom chromosome abnormalities such as deletion 20q, deletion 13q, or trisomy 9 occur in up to 30% of untreated PV patients, the JAK2 V617F mutation is more consistently associated with PV.
keyPoints: Nonrandom chromosome abnormalities occur in up to 30% of untreated PV patients.
JAK2 V617F mutation is found in >95% of PV patients and ~50% of PMF and ET patients.
Loss of heterozygosity on chromosome 9p involving the JAK2 locus occurs in ~60% of PV patients.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · POLYCYTHEMIA VERA | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Clinical Implications of JAK2 Mutations
items.content: The presence of JAK2 mutations, including JAK2 V617F and exon 12 mutations, influences the clinical presentation of PV but does not solely account for all phenotypic characteristics.
keyPoints: JAK2 V617F heterozygotes do not differ clinically from homozygotes.
PV patients without JAK2 V617F often have a mutation in exon 12, with no significant clinical differences except for isolated erythrocytosis.
ET and PMF patients can share the same mutations but exhibit different clinical phenotypes.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · POLYCYTHEMIA VERA | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Recognizing Polycythemia Vera (PV) through Clinical Features and Diagnostic Criteria
items.content: Polycythemia vera often presents with elevated hemoglobin, hematocrit, or red cell count. Key clinical features include neurologic symptoms from hyperviscosity, thrombosis in cerebral, cardiac, and mesenteric vessels, and complications like erythromelalgia due to increased platelet stickiness. Diagnostic evaluation is complex when PV presents with isolated erythrocytosis.
keyPoints: PV can present with high hemoglobin, hematocrit, or red cell count; thrombocytosis >900,000/mL causes acquired von Willebrand's disease.
Systolic hypertension and neurologic symptoms (vertigo, tinnitus, headache) are due to hyperviscosity from uncontrolled erythrocytosis.
PV should be suspected in women with hepatic vein thrombosis; JAK2 V617F expression is associated with this type of thrombosis.
Diagnostic evaluation for PV requires red cell mass and plasma volume determinations when hemoglobin < 20 g/dL or hematocrit < 60% to distinguish true from relative erythrocytosis.
JAK2 V617F or JAK2 exon 12 mutations are key diagnostic markers; normal serum erythropoietin does not exclude PV.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Complications and Thrombosis in Polycythemia Vera
items.content: PV can lead to thrombotic events, particularly affecting cerebral, cardiac, and mesenteric vessels. Hepatic venous thrombosis is common in young women with PV, while portal vein thrombosis is more frequent in males. Erythromelalgia results from increased platelet stickiness.
keyPoints: Thrombotic events are a major complication of PV, affecting cerebral, cardiac, and mesenteric vessels.
Hepatic venous thrombosis (Budd-Chiari syndrome) is common in young women with PV; portal vein thrombosis more common in males.
Erythromelalgia due to increased platelet stickiness is a complication of thrombocytosis in PV.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Diagnostic Approach for Isolated Erythrocytosis in Polycythemia Vera
items.content: When PV presents with isolated erythrocytosis, diagnostic evaluation involves assessing red cell mass and plasma volume to distinguish true from relative erythrocytosis. JAK2 V617F mutation testing is crucial, though not all expressing low allele burden have a blood disease.
keyPoints: Isolated erythrocytosis in PV requires red cell mass and plasma volume determinations if hemoglobin < 20 g/dL or hematocrit < 60%.
JAK2 V617F mutation testing is essential for diagnosis; not all with low allele burden have a blood disease.
Normal serum erythropoietin does not exclude PV, but elevated levels suggest secondary causes.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Understanding Thrombotic Risk in Polycythemia Vera (PV)
items.content: In PV, uncontrolled erythrocytosis can lead to thrombosis of vital organs. Massive splenomegaly complicates this risk by masking true red cell mass elevation due to increased plasma volume.
keyPoints: Thrombosis in PV involves vital organs such as the liver, heart, brain, or lungs.
Massive splenomegaly can mask red cell mass elevation, making a 'normal' hematocrit or hemoglobin level misleading.
A normal hematocrit or hemoglobin level in a patient with massive splenomegaly should be considered indicative of elevated red cell mass until proven otherwise.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS

items.title: Myelofibrosis and Its Prognostic Significance in PV
items.content: While myelofibrosis is common in PV, it is typically a reversible process without prognostic significance. However, in ~15% of patients, it leads to hematopoietic stem cell failure with significant clinical consequences.
keyPoints: Myelofibrosis is part of the natural history of PV and usually does not impede hematopoiesis or have prognostic significance.
~15% of PV patients develop myelofibrosis associated with hematopoietic stem cell failure.
This condition manifests as substantial extramedullary hematopoiesis in the liver and spleen, along with transfusion-dependent anemia.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS

items.title: Management of Hemoglobin Levels to Prevent Thrombosis
items.content: In Polycythemia Vera, maintaining specific hemoglobin levels is crucial to prevent thrombotic complications. Phlebotomy is used initially to reduce red cell mass and manage hyperviscosity.
keyPoints: Hemoglobin target: ≤140 g/L (14 g/dL) in men, ≤120 g/L (12 g/dL) in women
Hematocrit target: <45% in men, <42% in women
Phlebotomy reduces red cell mass and induces iron deficiency to prevent reexpansion
Once iron-deficient state is achieved, phlebotomies are typically required every 3 months
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera (part 1)

items.title: Thrombosis Management and Anticoagulation in PV
items.content: Anticoagulation is necessary following a thrombotic event in PV. Newer oral anticoagulants may be preferred over vitamin K antagonists due to lack of monitoring requirements.
keyPoints: Indication for anticoagulation: occurrence of thrombosis
Preference for newer oral anticoagulants over vitamin K antagonists
Salicylates are potentially harmful if red cell mass is not controlled and increase bleeding risk in patients over 60
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera (part 1)

items.title: Use of JAK Inhibitors for Symptom Management in PV
items.content: Ruxolitinib, a JAK1/2 inhibitor, is effective in reducing spleen size and alleviating symptoms in PV patients with myelofibrosis. It targets hematopoietic stem cells without mutagenicity.
keyPoints: Ruxolitinib reduces spleen size and constitutional symptoms
Effective for PV patients with or without myelofibrosis intolerant to hydroxyurea
Biweekly administration of pegylated IFN-α can produce complete remissions in ~20% of patients
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera (part 1)

items.title: Management of Red Cell Mass in Polycythemia Vera
items.content: In polycythemia vera, effective management of red cell mass primarily involves phlebotomy. Chemotherapy is not indicated for controlling red cell mass; however, ruxolitinib or pegylated interferon can be used when venous access poses a problem.
keyPoints: Phlebotomy alone can manage PV effectively without functional impairment.
Chemotherapy is never indicated to control red cell mass in PV.
Ruxolitinib or pegylated IFN are preferred therapies for managing venous access issues.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera (part 2) | PRIMARY MYELOFIBROSIS | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Genetic Mutations and Characterization of Primary Myelofibrosis
items.content: Primary myelofibrosis is characterized by marrow fibrosis, extramedullary hematopoiesis, splenomegaly, and mutations in JAK2, MPL, or CALR. The diagnosis can be challenging due to overlapping features with other disorders.
keyPoints: PMF involves mutations in JAK2 (~55%), MPL (~4%), or CALR.
No cytogenetic abnormality specific to PMF has been identified.
JAK2 V617F is present in ~55% of PMF patients.
MPL mutations occur in ~4% of PMF patients.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera (part 2) | PRIMARY MYELOFIBROSIS | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Recognizing PMF through Splenomegaly and Blood Count Abnormalities
items.content: Polycythemia Vera (PMF) is often detected incidentally during routine examinations due to splenic enlargement or abnormal blood counts, with many patients being asymptomatic at presentation.
keyPoints: Splenomegaly and/or abnormal blood counts are common initial findings in PMF.
Many patients present without symptoms; the disease may be discovered incidentally.
Characteristic blood smear features include teardrop-shaped red cells, nucleated red cells, myelocytes, promyelocytes, and possibly myeloblasts.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: Identifying Complications of PMF: Extramedullary Hematopoiesis and Hyperuricemia
items.content: Extramedullary hematopoiesis in PMF can lead to significant complications such as ascites, portal hypertension, and splenic infarction. Hyperuricemia may also occur, leading to secondary gout.
keyPoints: Extramedullary hematopoiesis can cause complications like ascites, portal hypertension, pulmonary or intracranial hypertension, intestinal or ureteral obstruction, pericardial tamponade, spinal cord compression, and skin nodules.
Splenic enlargement may lead to splenic infarction with fever and pleuritic chest pain.
Hyperuricemia can result in secondary gout.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: Diagnosis of Primary Myelofibrosis (PMF)
items.content: The diagnosis of PMF is one of exclusion, requiring ruling out other disorders. Key diagnostic features include teardrop-shaped red cells and increased marrow reticulin, with a hypercellular marrow showing trilineage hyperplasia and dysplastic megakaryocytes.
keyPoints: Diagnosis requires exclusion of other myeloproliferative neoplasms (MPNs).
Teardrop-shaped red cells and nucleated red cells indicate extramedullary hematopoiesis.
Marrow biopsy shows hypercellular marrow with trilineage hyperplasia and dysplastic megakaryocytes.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Role of JAK2 V617F Mutation in PMF Diagnosis
items.content: Approximately 55% of PMF patients express the JAK2 V617F mutation, often as homozygotes. Patients with this mutation tend to be older and have higher hematocrits compared to those with MPL or CALR mutations.
keyPoints: ~55% of PMF patients express the JAK2 V617F mutation.
JAK2 V617F-positive patients are usually older with higher hematocrits.
MPL and CALR mutations present different clinical features.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Prognostic Scoring Systems in PMF
items.content: The DIPSS Plus scoring system, which includes unfavorable karyotype, thrombocytopenia, and transfusion dependence, improves risk stratification. Mutational analysis of ASXL1, EZH2, SRSF2, and IDH1/2 genes further refines prognosis.
keyPoints: DIPSS Plus assigns additional points for unfavorable karyotype, thrombocytopenia, and transfusion dependence.
Mutational analysis of ASXL1, EZH2, SRSF2, and IDH1/2 improves risk stratification.
Cytogenetic abnormalities are important for prognosis.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Prognostic Factors for Disease Acceleration in PMF
items.content: In primary myelofibrosis (PMF), certain risk factors are associated with disease acceleration and poorer prognosis. These include complex cytogenetic abnormalities, thrombocytopenia, transfusion-dependent anemia, and specific gene mutations.
keyPoints: Complex cytogenetic abnormalities, thrombocytopenia, and transfusion-dependent anemia are prognostic factors for disease acceleration.
Mutations in ASXL1, EZH2, SRSF2, and IDH1/2 genes are risk factors for early death or transformation to acute leukemia.
These genetic mutations are more useful for PMF risk assessment than clinical scoring systems.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Primary Myelofibrosis

items.title: Management of Anemia in PMF
items.content: Anemia in primary myelofibrosis is multifactorial and challenging to treat. Erythropoietin therapy is ineffective if serum levels exceed 125 mU/L, while glucocorticoids can help ameliorate anemia and constitutional symptoms.
keyPoints: Causes of anemia include ineffective erythropoiesis, splenic sequestration, hemodilution, blood loss, folic acid deficiency, systemic inflammation, and autoimmune hemolysis.
Erythropoietin is ineffective if serum levels are >125 mU/L.
Glucocorticoids can improve anemia and constitutional symptoms.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Primary Myelofibrosis

items.title: Treatment Options for Splenomegaly in PMF
items.content: Splenomegaly is a major complication in PMF, causing significant morbidity. Treatment options include ruxolitinib to reduce splenomegaly and alleviate symptoms, but it may cause anemia and thrombocytopenia.
keyPoints: Ruxolitinib reduces splenomegaly and alleviates constitutional symptoms in advanced PMF patients.
Anemia and thrombocytopenia are dose-dependent side effects of ruxolitinib.
Splenectomy is associated with significant postoperative complications and increases the risk of blastic transformation.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Primary Myelofibrosis

items.title: Understanding the Etiology of Essential Thrombocythemia (ET)
items.content: Essential thrombocythemia is a clonal hematopoietic stem cell disorder characterized by overproduction of platelets due to mutations in JAK2, MPL, or CALR. It has an incidence of 1–2/100,000 with a distinct female predominance.
keyPoints: ET is associated with mutations in JAK2 (V617F), MPL, or CALR.
Incidence: 1–2/100,000
Distinct female predominance noted.
Approximately 50% of ET patients express JAK2 V617F, 30% CALR, and 8% MPL mutations.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · ESSENTIAL THROMBOCYTOSIS | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Role of Thrombopoietin in Megakaryocytopoiesis
items.content: Thrombopoietin, produced only in the liver, is crucial for maintaining hematopoietic stem cells quiescent and promoting proliferation once they are released from niches. It also enhances platelet reactivity.
keyPoints: Thrombopoietin maintains hematopoietic stem cell quiescence in endosteal niches.
Promotes proliferation of these cells in sinusoidal niches upon release.
Enhances the reactivity of mature platelets.
Plasma thrombopoietin level inversely correlates with platelet count.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · ESSENTIAL THROMBOCYTOSIS | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Distinguishing Essential Thrombocythemia from Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms (MPNs)
items.content: Clinical and laboratory criteria are used to distinguish ET from other MPNs, which may present with thrombocytosis but have different prognoses and treatments.
keyPoints: ET must be distinguished from CML, PV, PMF, or myelodysplasia.
Canonical MPN driver mutations help differentiate ET from reactive forms of thrombocytosis.
Mutation-negative ET patients may have uncommon MPL mutations or hereditary thrombocytosis.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · ESSENTIAL THROMBOCYTOSIS | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Recognition of Essential Thrombocythemia (ET) in Routine Evaluations
items.content: Essential thrombocythemia is often identified incidentally during routine medical evaluations when a platelet count is obtained. Reviewing past blood counts may reveal previously overlooked elevated platelet levels.
keyPoints: ET can be identified incidentally during routine evaluations.
Review of previous blood counts might show overlooked elevated platelet counts.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: Distinguishing Features and Diagnostic Considerations in ET
items.content: Patients with essential thrombocythemia may present with hemorrhagic or thrombotic tendencies, such as easy bruising or microvascular occlusive events. Splenomegaly suggests another myeloproliferative neoplasm (MPN). Marrow biopsy typically shows megakaryocyte hypertrophy and hyperplasia.
keyPoints: Patients may have hemorrhagic signs like easy bruising, or thrombotic signs such as erythromelalgia, ocular migraine, or TIA.
Splenomegaly suggests another MPN (e.g., PV, PMF, CML).
Marrow biopsy usually reveals megakaryocyte hypertrophy and hyperplasia.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: JAK2 V617F Mutation in Thrombocytosis Diagnosis
items.content: In patients with thrombocytosis, the presence of JAK2 V617F mutation is found in about 50% of ET cases. When absent, further cytogenetic evaluation is necessary to differentiate between CML and other myelodysplastic disorders.
keyPoints: JAK2 V617F mutation present in ~50% of ET patients
Cytogenetic evaluation required if JAK2 V617F is absent to rule out CML or myelodysplastic disorders
BCR-ABL FISH analysis preferred over RT-PCR due to false positives
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Distinguishing ET from PV Using JAK2 V617F Neutrophil Allele Burden
items.content: The quantitative JAK2 V617F neutrophil allele burden cannot reliably distinguish between ET and PV due to overlap, except that in ET it is never greater than 50%. Red cell mass and plasma volume determinations are crucial as they can reveal underlying PV.
keyPoints: JAK2 V617F neutrophil allele burden overlaps between ET and PV
In ET, JAK2 V617F neutrophil allele burden is never >50%
64% of JAK2 V617F-positive ET patients were found to have PV upon red cell mass and plasma volume determinations
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Thrombocytosis and Thrombosis Risk
items.content: Contrary to common belief, high platelet counts do not necessarily cause thrombosis. In patients under 60 years old with thrombocytosis, the incidence of thrombosis is no greater than in age-matched controls. Tobacco use is a more significant risk factor for thrombosis in these patients.
keyPoints: No controlled clinical study establishes high platelet counts as causing thrombosis.
Incidence of thrombosis not higher in ET patients under 60 compared to controls.
Tobacco use is the most important risk factor for thrombosis in ET patients.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Essential Thrombocytosis

items.title: Management of Symptoms in ET Patients
items.content: Symptoms in ET patients should be managed based on their specific characteristics rather than solely on platelet count. For instance, migraines may require lowering the platelet count, while erythromelalgia can be treated with cyclooxygenase-1 inhibitors like aspirin or ibuprofen without reducing platelet numbers.
keyPoints: Focus should be on patient symptoms rather than just platelet count.
Migraines in ET may respond to lowering platelet count.
Erythromelalgia can be treated with cyclooxygenase-1 inhibitors.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Essential Thrombocytosis

items.title: Therapeutic Approach for High Platelet Counts in ET
items.content: In asymptomatic ET patients without cardiovascular risk factors or tobacco use, an elevated platelet count does not require therapy. For symptomatic patients, identify if symptoms are due to high platelet counts before initiating treatment. Aspirin may promote hemorrhage when platelet counts exceed $1 	imes 10^6/μ L$.
keyPoints: Asymptomatic ET patients without risk factors do not require therapy for elevated platelets.
Symptoms must be linked to high platelet count before treatment.
Aspirin can promote hemorrhage in counts above $1 	imes 10^6/μ L$.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Essential Thrombocytosis

items.subtopic: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms
items.question: Which genetic mutation is commonly associated with polycythemia vera (PV), primary myelofibrosis (PMF), and essential thrombocytosis (ET)?
items.explanation: PV, PMF, and ET are characterized by JAK2 driver mutations.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms

items.subtopic: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms
items.question: Which of the following myeloproliferative neoplasms is associated with a balanced translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22?
items.explanation: CML is associated with the t(9;22) translocation.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms

items.subtopic: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms
items.question: Which of these disorders primarily express a myeloid phenotype?
items.explanation: CML expresses primarily a myeloid phenotype.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms

items.question: Which mutation is most commonly associated with Polycythemia Vera (PV) pathogenesis?
items.explanation: The JAK2 V617F mutation is central to PV pathogenesis, affecting over 95% of patients.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · POLYCYTHEMIA VERA | ETIOLOGY

items.question: What role does the JAK2 gene play in normal physiology?
options: It acts as a growth factor for red blood cells.
It serves as a tyrosine kinase for erythropoietin and thrombopoietin receptors.
It is responsible for platelet aggregation.
It regulates white blood cell count.
items.explanation: JAK2 functions as the cognate tyrosine kinase for the erythropoietin and thrombopoietin receptors.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · POLYCYTHEMIA VERA | ETIOLOGY

items.question: What is a common cytogenetic abnormality in PV patients over time?
items.explanation: Loss of heterozygosity on chromosome 9p involving the JAK2 locus is common in PV patients.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · POLYCYTHEMIA VERA | ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which statement about JAK2 mutations in MPNs is correct?
options: JAK2 V617F mutation accounts for the entire phenotype of all MPNs.
Most PV patients without JAK2 V617F have a different clinical presentation.
JAK2 V617F heterozygotes and homozygotes differ clinically.
Approximately 50% of PMF and ET patients express JAK2 V617F.
items.explanation: Most PV patients who do not have the JAK2 V617F mutation have a similar clinical presentation, except for isolated erythrocytosis.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · POLYCYTHEMIA VERA | ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which symptom complex in polycythemia vera (PV) is due to increased platelet stickiness?
items.explanation: Erythromelalgia in PV is due to increased platelet stickiness.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: In the context of polycythemia vera (PV), which type of thrombosis should raise suspicion for the disease, especially when associated with JAK2 V617F expression?
items.explanation: PV should be suspected in any woman who develops hepatic vein thrombosis, associated with JAK2 V617F expression.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the most common complication of extreme thrombocytosis (>900,000/mL) in polycythemia vera (PV)?
items.explanation: Extreme thrombocytosis in PV can cause acquired von Willebrand's disease due to absorption and proteolysis of high-molecular-weight von Willebrand multimers.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which diagnostic method has superseded other surrogate tests for establishing the diagnosis of polycythemia vera (PV)?
options: Red cell mass and plasma volume determinations
Quantitative assay for JAK2 V617F or JAK2 exon 12 mutations
items.explanation: The quantitative assay for JAK2 V617F or JAK2 exon 12 mutations using next-generation sequencing technology has superseded other surrogate tests.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which complication is associated with the activation of mast cells by JAK2 V617F in polycythemia vera (PV)?
items.explanation: Pruritus in PV may be a consequence of mast cell activation by JAK2 V617F.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS

items.question: What is the significance of myelofibrosis in the natural history of polycythemia vera (PV)?
options: It impairs hematopoiesis and has prognostic significance.
It is a reactive, reversible process with no prognostic significance by itself.
It leads to immediate transformation into acute myeloid leukemia.
It causes substantial extramedullary hematopoiesis in all patients.
items.explanation: Myelofibrosis is a reactive, reversible process that does not impede hematopoiesis and has no prognostic significance by itself in PV.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS

items.question: What should be considered when a patient with polycythemia vera (PV) presents with 'normal' hematocrit or hemoglobin levels but massive splenomegaly?
options: The red cell mass is likely decreased.
The plasma volume increase masks the true extent of red cell mass elevation.
There is no need for further investigation.
It indicates a low risk of thrombotic events.
items.explanation: 'Normal' hematocrit or hemoglobin levels in PV with massive splenomegaly should be considered indicative of an elevated red cell mass due to plasma volume increase masking the true extent.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS

items.question: What is the recommended maximum hemoglobin level to avoid thrombotic complications in men with Polycythemia Vera?
items.explanation: Maintaining a hemoglobin level at ≤140 g/L is mandatory to avoid thrombotic complications in men.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera (part 1)

items.question: Which treatment is preferred over vitamin K antagonists for anticoagulation after a thrombosis in PV patients?
items.explanation: The newer oral anticoagulants may be preferable to vitamin K antagonists as they do not require monitoring.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera (part 1)

items.question: Which therapy is recommended for asymptomatic hyperuricemia in PV patients?
items.explanation: Asymptomatic hyperuricemia requires no therapy unless there is a risk of further elevation due to chemotherapy.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera (part 1)

items.question: Which drug should be avoided in PV patients due to its leukemogenic potential?
items.explanation: Hydroxyurea is leukemogenic and should be considered as short-duration therapy.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera (part 1)

items.question: Which therapy is preferred when venous access is an issue in managing polycythemia vera?
items.explanation: Ruxolitinib or pegylated IFN is preferred when venous access is an issue in PV.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera (part 2) | PRIMARY MYELOFIBROSIS | ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following mutations are associated with primary myelofibrosis?
items.explanation: Mutations in JAK2, MPL, or CALR are associated with primary myelofibrosis.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera (part 2) | PRIMARY MYELOFIBROSIS | ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which statement is true regarding the etiology of primary myelofibrosis?
options: JAK2 V617F mutation is present in ~90% of PMF patients.
MPL mutations occur in ~50% of PMF patients.
CALR mutations alter the carboxyterminal portion of the protein to activate MPL.
Myelofibrosis and extramedullary hematopoiesis are directly related.
items.explanation: Most CALR mutations in PMF alter the carboxyterminal portion, allowing it to bind and activate MPL.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera (part 2) | PRIMARY MYELOFIBROSIS | ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following is a common presenting complaint in PMF that differentiates it from its companion MPN?
options: Night sweats, fatigue, and weight loss
items.explanation: Night sweats, fatigue, and weight loss are common presenting complaints in PMF, unlike its companion MPN.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: What is a characteristic feature seen on a blood smear in patients with PMF?
options: Teardrop-shaped red cells and nucleated red cells
items.explanation: A blood smear in PMF shows teardrop-shaped red cells, nucleated red cells, and other immature forms.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: Which condition is suggested by isolated lymphadenopathy in the context of PMF?
items.explanation: Isolated lymphadenopathy should suggest another diagnosis, not PMF.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: Which of the following findings is indicative of extramedullary hematopoiesis in polycythemia vera (PV)?
options: Leukocytosis and thrombocytosis with large and bizarre platelets
Presence of teardrop-shaped red cells, nucleated red cells, myelocytes, and promyelocytes
Increased marrow reticulin making the marrow inaspirable
Splenomegaly due to extramedullary hematopoiesis causing portal hypertension
items.explanation: The presence of teardrop-shaped red cells, nucleated red cells, myelocytes, and promyelocytes establishes the presence of extramedullary hematopoiesis.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: In polycythemia vera (PV), what can massive splenomegaly mask?
items.explanation: Massive splenomegaly commonly masks erythrocytosis in PV.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the significance of a low JAK2 V617F mutation allele burden (≤25%) in PMF patients?
options: It indicates a better prognosis with slower progression.
It suggests faster time to anemia and leukopenia, and shortened survival.
It shows resistance to standard therapies for MPNs.
It implies the presence of extramedullary hematopoiesis.
items.explanation: A low JAK2 V617F mutation allele burden (≤25%) in PMF patients is associated with a faster time to anemia and leukopenia, and shortened survival.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which of the following mutations are found in a majority of PMF patients who lack JAK2 or MPL mutations?
items.explanation: Somatic mutations in exon 9 of CALR are found in a majority of PMF patients who lack JAK2 or MPL mutations.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a cause for anemia in patients with primary myelofibrosis?
options: Ineffective erythropoiesis uncompensated by splenic extramedullary hematopoiesis
items.explanation: Anemia in PMF is not caused by increased serum erythropoietin levels; rather, it's ineffective if the level is >125 mU/L.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Primary Myelofibrosis

items.question: Which treatment has been shown to be effective in reducing splenomegaly and alleviating constitutional symptoms in advanced PMF patients?
items.explanation: Ruxolitinib has been effective in reducing splenomegaly and alleviating symptoms in advanced PMF patients.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Primary Myelofibrosis

items.question: Which of the following is a risk factor for early death or transformation to acute leukemia in primary myelofibrosis?
items.explanation: Mutations in the ASXL1 gene are risk factors for early death or transformation to acute leukemia.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Primary Myelofibrosis

items.question: Which of the following is a potential complication associated with splenectomy in PMF patients?
options: Decrease in risk of blastic transformation
items.explanation: Splenectomy is associated with complications such as mesenteric venous thrombosis.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Primary Myelofibrosis

items.question: Which mutation is found in approximately 50% of patients with essential thrombocythemia (ET)?
items.explanation: Approximately 50% of ET patients express the JAK2 V617F mutation.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · ESSENTIAL THROMBOCYTOSIS | ETIOLOGY

items.question: What is a unique feature of megakaryocyte genome replication in hematopoiesis?
options: It occurs only in the bone marrow
items.explanation: Megakaryocytes undergo endomitotic genome replication, which is unique among hematopoietic progenitor cells.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · ESSENTIAL THROMBOCYTOSIS | ETIOLOGY

items.question: Where is thrombopoietin primarily produced?
items.explanation: Thrombopoietin is produced only in the liver.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · ESSENTIAL THROMBOCYTOSIS | ETIOLOGY

items.question: What is the relationship between plasma thrombopoietin levels and platelet count?
options: Platelet count does not affect plasma thrombopoietin
items.explanation: There is an inverse correlation between the platelet count and plasma thrombopoietin.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · ESSENTIAL THROMBOCYTOSIS | ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following symptoms is NOT specifically associated with Essential Thrombocythemia (ET)?
items.explanation: Splenomegaly is indicative of other myeloproliferative neoplasms, not specifically ET.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: What can hinder the accurate measurement of serum potassium in patients with Essential Thrombocythemia (ET)?
options: Release of platelet potassium upon blood clotting
items.explanation: The release of platelet potassium upon blood clotting can hinder the accurate measurement of serum potassium.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: What is a common finding on a blood smear in patients with Essential Thrombocythemia (ET)?
items.explanation: A blood smear in ET is most remarkable for the number and size of platelets present.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: Which mutation is found in about 50% of Essential Thrombocythemia (ET) patients?
items.explanation: About 50% of ET patients express the JAK2 V617F mutation.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: In patients with thrombocytosis and a negative cytogenetic study for the Ph chromosome, which assay is preferred to detect BCR-ABL?
items.explanation: Fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) analysis for BCR-ABL is the preferred assay when the Ph chromosome study is negative.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which of the following conditions can evolve into Polycythemia Vera (PV) or Primary Myelofibrosis (PMF) due to clonal evolution?
items.explanation: What appears to be ET can evolve into PV or PMF due to clonal evolution.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which factor has been identified as the most important risk for thrombosis in patients with essential thrombocythemia (ET)?
items.explanation: Tobacco use is the most important risk factor for thrombosis in ET patients.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Essential Thrombocytosis

items.question: What condition can result from very high platelet counts due to the removal and destruction of high-molecular-weight von Willebrand multimers?
items.explanation: Very high platelet counts can lead to acquired von Willebrand's disease.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Essential Thrombocytosis

items.question: What is the recommended treatment for bleeding due to acquired von Willebrand's disease in ET patients?
items.explanation: Bleeding in acquired von Willebrand's disease usually responds to tranexamic acid or ε-aminocaproic acid.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Essential Thrombocytosis

items.question: Which therapy for ET patients is associated with an increased risk of developing acute leukemia without proven benefit?
items.explanation: ET patients treated with $^{32}P$ or alkylating agents are at risk of developing acute leukemia without any proof of benefit.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Essential Thrombocytosis

items.subtopic: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms
items.statement: Transformation to acute leukemia is common in chronic myelogenous leukemia (CML) without chemotherapy.
items.explanation: CML has a high rate of leukemic transformation.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms

items.subtopic: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms
items.statement: Primary myelofibrosis (PMF) is characterized by mutations in the CSF3R gene.
items.explanation: CSF3R mutation is associated with chronic neutrophilic leukemia, not PMF.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms

items.statement: JAK2 mutations are mutually exclusive with other MPN driver mutations.
items.explanation: MPN driver mutations, including JAK2, LNK, and CALR, can coexist.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · POLYCYTHEMIA VERA | ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Familial PV always involves the JAK2 V617F mutation.
items.explanation: Familial PV can occur without the JAK2 V617F mutation.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · POLYCYTHEMIA VERA | ETIOLOGY

items.statement: A normal serum erythropoietin level excludes the presence of polycythemia vera (PV).
items.explanation: A normal serum erythropoietin level does not exclude PV; an elevated level suggests a secondary cause.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: In polycythemia vera (PV), cerebral, cardiac, and mesenteric vessels are commonly involved in thrombosis.
items.explanation: Cerebral, cardiac, and mesenteric vessels are most commonly affected by venous or arterial thrombosis in PV.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: The incidence of acute myeloid leukemia is significantly increased in PV patients who have not been exposed to chemotherapy or radiation therapy.
items.explanation: The incidence of acute leukemia in PV patients not exposed to chemotherapy or radiation therapy is very low.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS

items.statement: Erythromelalgia, associated with thrombocytosis, typically involves the upper extremities.
items.explanation: Erythromelalgia primarily involves the lower extremities.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS

items.statement: Thrombocytosis or leukocytosis in PV patients is strongly correlated with thrombosis.
items.explanation: Neither thrombocytosis nor leukocytosis is correlated with thrombosis; only erythrocytosis is.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera (part 1)

items.statement: Ruxolitinib can reduce spleen size in PV patients complicated by myelofibrosis.
items.explanation: Ruxolitinib has been effective in reducing spleen size and alleviating symptoms in such cases.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera (part 1)

items.statement: Chemotherapy is indicated to control the red cell mass in polycythemia vera.
items.explanation: Chemotherapy is never indicated for controlling red cell mass in PV.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera (part 2) | PRIMARY MYELOFIBROSIS | ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Fibroblasts in primary myelofibrosis are part of the neoplastic clone.
items.explanation: Fibroblasts in PMF are polyclonal and not part of the neoplastic clone.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera (part 2) | PRIMARY MYELOFIBROSIS | ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Mild hepatomegaly is common and usually accompanies splenomegaly in PMF.
items.explanation: Mild hepatomegaly may accompany splenomegaly but is unusual in its absence.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.statement: Bone x-rays in PMF patients typically show osteopenia.
items.explanation: Bone x-rays in PMF may reveal osteosclerosis, not osteopenia.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.statement: The diagnosis of primary myelofibrosis (PMF) is one of exclusion.
items.explanation: Diagnosis of PMF requires ruling out other disorders with overlapping features.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Cytogenetic analysis in PMF patients is only useful for excluding CML.
items.explanation: Cytogenetic analysis is also used for prognostic purposes, as complex karyotype abnormalities indicate poor prognosis.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Allogeneic bone marrow transplantation is the only curative treatment for PMF.
items.explanation: Bone marrow transplantation is indeed the only curative option for PMF.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Primary Myelofibrosis

items.statement: Ruxolitinib is associated with no side effects in PMF patients.
items.explanation: Ruxolitinib can cause anemia and thrombocytopenia as major side effects.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Primary Myelofibrosis

items.statement: Thrombopoietin production occurs in both the liver and bone marrow.
items.explanation: Thrombopoietin is produced only in the liver.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · ESSENTIAL THROMBOCYTOSIS | ETIOLOGY

items.statement: ET can occur at any age and often without symptoms.
items.explanation: ET can indeed occur at any adult age and often presents without symptoms.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · ESSENTIAL THROMBOCYTOSIS | ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Splenomegaly is a common finding in Essential Thrombocythemia.
items.explanation: Splenomegaly indicates another myeloproliferative neoplasm, not specifically ET.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.statement: An elevated platelet count can cause hyperkalemia due to a test tube artifact.
items.explanation: The release of platelet potassium upon clotting can lead to a test tube artifact causing hyperkalemia.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.statement: The absolute level of the platelet count is a useful diagnostic aid for distinguishing between benign and clonal causes of thrombocytosis.
items.explanation: The absolute level of the platelet count is not a useful diagnostic aid for this purpose.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Anemia and ringed sideroblasts are features of Essential Thrombocythemia (ET).
items.explanation: Anemia and ringed sideroblasts are not features of ET.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Normalizing the platelet count prevents both arterial and venous thrombosis in ET patients.
items.explanation: Normalizing the platelet count does not prevent either arterial or venous thrombosis.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Essential Thrombocytosis

items.statement: ET is more likely to evolve into acute leukemia due to therapy rather than the disease itself.
items.explanation: Evolution to acute leukemia in ET is more likely a consequence of therapy.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Essential Thrombocytosis

items.assertion: JAK2 V617F is responsible for erythropoietin hypersensitivity in PV.
items.reason: Constitutive activation of JAK2 explains the erythropoietin-independent erythroid colony formation and increased Bcl-XL expression.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · POLYCYTHEMIA VERA | ETIOLOGY

items.assertion: ET and PMF patients exhibit different clinical phenotypes despite having similar mutations.
items.reason: JAK2 V617F mutation accounts for all phenotypic differences in MPNs.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason is incorrect as JAK2 V617F alone does not account for all clinical differences.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · POLYCYTHEMIA VERA | ETIOLOGY

items.assertion: Polycythemia vera (PV) can be diagnosed solely based on elevated hemoglobin and hematocrit levels.
items.reason: Elevated hemoglobin and hematocrit alone do not distinguish true erythrocytosis from plasma volume contraction disorders.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because red cell mass and plasma volume determinations are necessary to establish absolute erythrocytosis.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Hepatic venous thrombosis in young women should prompt consideration of polycythemia vera (PV).
items.reason: This type of thrombosis is associated with JAK2 V617F expression in PV.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why hepatic venous thrombosis should prompt consideration of PV.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Phlebotomy is used to reduce hyperviscosity in PV patients.
items.reason: Phlebotomy reduces the red cell mass to normal and expands plasma volume.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera (part 1)

items.assertion: Anagrelide is preferred over hydroxyurea for reducing platelet count in PV.
items.reason: Anagrelide lacks marrow toxicity and protects against venous thrombosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why anagrelide is preferred.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera (part 1)

items.assertion: Myelofibrosis in primary myelofibrosis is associated with overproduction of transforming growth factor $ \beta $.
items.reason: Osteosclerosis in PMF is linked to osteoprotegerin overproduction.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · Polycythemia Vera (part 2) | PRIMARY MYELOFIBROSIS | ETIOLOGY

items.assertion: PMF patients with a low JAK2 V617F mutation allele burden have a better survival rate.
items.reason: A low JAK2 V617F mutation allele burden is associated with faster progression to anemia and leukopenia, leading to shortened survival.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the reason correctly explains that a low JAK2 V617F mutation allele burden leads to worse outcomes.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Triple-negative PMF patients have the best prognosis.
items.reason: PMF patients lacking known MPN driver mutations (triple-negative) appear to have the worst prognosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the reason correctly states that triple-negative PMF patients have the worst prognosis.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Pegylated IFN-α ameliorates fibrosis in advanced PMF.
items.reason: Pegylated IFN-α may exacerbate bone marrow failure in advanced disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; pegylated IFN-α can ameliorate fibrosis in early, not advanced, PMF.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Primary Myelofibrosis

items.assertion: Splenic irradiation provides a long-term solution for splenomegaly in PMF.
items.reason: Splenic irradiation is associated with significant risks of neutropenia and infection.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; splenic irradiation is only temporarily palliative.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Primary Myelofibrosis

items.assertion: The clonal nature of essential thrombocythemia was established by glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase isoenzyme analysis.
items.reason: Essential thrombocythemia involves a clonal hematopoietic stem cell disorder.
items.explanation: The assertion and reason are both true, and the reason explains why glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase isoenzyme analysis was used to establish ET's clonality.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · ESSENTIAL THROMBOCYTOSIS | ETIOLOGY

items.assertion: Essential thrombocythemia has a distinct male predominance.
items.reason: ET is associated with mutations in JAK2, MPL, or CALR.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as there is an unexplained female predominance; the reason is true but does not relate to sex distribution.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · ESSENTIAL THROMBOCYTOSIS | ETIOLOGY

items.assertion: ET patients with asymptomatic thrombocytosis and no cardiovascular risk factors require no therapy.
items.reason: Asymptomatic ET patients without cardiovascular risks or tobacco use do not need therapy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Essential Thrombocytosis

items.assertion: Aspirin is effective in preventing all types of thrombosis in ET patients.
items.reason: Hydroxyurea combined with aspirin was more effective than anagrelide and aspirin for the prevention of TIA.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because normalizing platelet count does not prevent all types of thrombosis, while the reason is true regarding hydroxyurea's effectiveness in preventing TIAs.
items.reference: Polycythemia Vera and Other Myeloproliferative Neoplasms · COMPLICATIONS | Essential Thrombocytosis
